# DEV.TO ARTICLE 1

**PLATFORM:** dev.to / Hashnode / Medium
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Title:** i built 10 free SaaS tools with zero backend infrastructure
**Tags:** `webdev` `javascript` `pwa` `saas`
**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)
**Cover image alt:** browser tabs showing 10 deployed SaaS tools, all running client-side
**Target length:** 800-1200 words

---

10 SaaS tools. zero servers. $0/mo running cost. all deployed and working right now.

no database. no auth server. no AWS bill. no Docker containers. every tool runs entirely in the browser using vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. deployed on surge.sh in under 30 seconds each.

here are all 10 with live URLs.

## the tools

**1. SiteScore** - website auditor
https://sitescore-free.surge.sh
runs Lighthouse-style checks client-side. scores your site on performance, SEO, accessibility, and security headers. exports PDF reports.

**2. PageScorer** - page speed scorer
https://pagescorer-app.surge.sh
paste a URL, get a speed breakdown. tracks core web vitals, resource loading, DOM weight. no API key needed because it runs the checks in your browser.

**3. ColdMaxx** - cold email toolkit
https://coldmaxx-app.surge.sh
generates cold email sequences from templates. 6-question framework baked in. handles follow-up timing, subject line variations, and personalization tokens. all stored in localStorage.

**4. InvoiceForge** - invoice generator
https://invoiceforge-app.surge.sh
fill in the fields, hit generate, download PDF. stores your company details locally so you don't re-enter them. line items, tax rates, notes, payment terms.

**5. ContentCalendar** - content calendar
https://contentcalendar.surge.sh
drag-and-drop content planning. 30-day view. platform tags for Twitter, LinkedIn, YouTube, newsletter. exports to CSV. all state in localStorage.

**6. Social Dashboard** - social metrics tracker
https://social-dashboard-pm.surge.sh
manual entry social dashboard. track followers, engagement rates, post performance across platforms. charts render with Chart.js. data persists in browser storage.

**7. ROI Calculator** - marketing ROI calculator
https://roicalc-app.surge.sh
plug in ad spend, conversion rate, customer LTV. get back CAC, ROAS, break-even timeline. save scenarios for comparison.

**8. ProspectMaxx** - prospect finder
https://prospectmaxx-app.surge.sh
build prospect lists manually with scoring. fields for company, contact, email, LinkedIn, notes, lead score. filter and sort. export CSV.

**9. StackMaxx** - tech stack builder
https://stackmaxx-app.surge.sh
pick your stack components from categorized lists (frontend, backend, database, hosting, CI/CD). generates a tech stack document with cost estimates and integration notes.

**10. PitchDeck Builder** - pitch deck builder
https://pitchdeck-app.surge.sh
slide-by-slide pitch deck creator. follows the standard 10-slide format (problem, solution, market, traction, team, ask). exports to printable HTML.

## the tech stack (there isn't one)

every tool follows the same pattern:

```html
<!-- index.html -->
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>ToolName</title>
  <link rel="manifest" href="manifest.json">
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>
  <div id="app"></div>
  <script src="app.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

```json
// manifest.json - makes it installable as PWA
{
  "name": "ToolName",
  "short_name": "Tool",
  "start_url": "/",
  "display": "standalone",
  "background_color": "#ffffff",
  "theme_color": "#000000",
  "icons": [
    { "src": "icon-192.png", "sizes": "192x192", "type": "image/png" },
    { "src": "icon-512.png", "sizes": "512x512", "type": "image/png" }
  ]
}
```

```javascript
// sw.js - service worker for offline support
const CACHE_NAME = 'tool-v1';
const ASSETS = ['/', '/index.html', '/style.css', '/app.js'];

self.addEventListener('install', e => {
  e.waitUntil(caches.open(CACHE_NAME).then(c => c.addAll(ASSETS)));
});

self.addEventListener('fetch', e => {
  e.respondWith(caches.match(e.request).then(r => r || fetch(e.request)));
});
```

that's it. three files plus your app logic. no bundler, no transpiler, no node_modules folder with 847 packages.

## why this works

**localStorage is your database.** for single-user tools like calculators, generators, and planners, you don't need a backend. the user's browser stores everything. no database to maintain, no auth to implement, no GDPR headaches about server-side data storage.

**surge.sh is your host.** `npm install -g surge` then `surge .` in your project folder. free tier gives you unlimited projects with custom subdomains. deploy takes 10-15 seconds.

**PWA manifest makes it installable.** users can add it to their home screen. it works offline. it feels like a native app. zero app store approval process.

## the tradeoffs (being honest)

- no multi-device sync. your data lives in one browser on one device.
- no collaboration. these are single-user tools.
- localStorage has a ~5MB limit. fine for most use cases, breaks if you're storing thousands of records.
- no real analytics beyond what surge provides (which is nothing).

these tools aren't replacing Notion or Figma. they're replacing the spreadsheet you were going to use anyway.

## the real insight

the cheapest infrastructure is no infrastructure.

i see devs spin up a Postgres instance, a Redis cache, and a Kubernetes cluster to build a to-do app. the monthly bill hits $40 before a single user signs up.

every one of these 10 tools has zero recurring cost. if nobody uses them, i lose nothing. if 10,000 people use them, i still lose nothing. the risk is zero because the cost is zero.

the 10 tools took about 3 days total to build. most of the time was spent on UI, not infrastructure. because there is no infrastructure.

just build the thing. put it on surge. move on to the next one.

# DEV.TO ARTICLE 2

**PLATFORM:** dev.to / Hashnode / Medium
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Title:** the streak factory: how i generated 28 habit-tracking apps from one template
**Tags:** `javascript` `webdev` `productivity` `opensource`
**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)
**Cover image alt:** grid of 28 app icons showing different streak tracker variants
**Target length:** 800-1200 words

---

1 template. 28 apps. 6 religions. 7 hobbies. 14 denominations. all deployed and running.

i built a single habit-tracking PWA template, then generated 28 variants by swapping variables. colors, names, scripture sources, tracking categories. the template stays the same. the config changes.

total time from template to 28 deployed apps: about 4 hours.

## the base template

the original is scripture-streak. a PWA that tracks daily scripture reading habits with streaks, progress bars, and reading plans.

the core template has 4 files:

```
scripture-streak/
  index.html      (shell + layout)
  style.css       (theme variables at the top)
  app.js          (streak logic, localStorage, UI rendering)
  manifest.json   (PWA config)
```

the streak logic is identical across all 28 apps. what changes is the config object at the top of app.js:

```javascript
const CONFIG = {
  appName: "Scripture Streak",
  primaryColor: "#2D5016",
  accentColor: "#4A7C23",
  categories: ["Old Testament", "New Testament", "Psalms", "Proverbs"],
  dailyGoal: 1,
  streakUnit: "chapters",
  motivationalQuotes: [
    "Your word is a lamp for my feet, a light on my path.",
    // ...
  ],
  icon: "bible-icon.png"
};
```

## how variable injection works

to make a new app, i change the CONFIG object. that's it.

buddhist-streak:

```javascript
const CONFIG = {
  appName: "Buddhist Streak",
  primaryColor: "#FF8C00",
  accentColor: "#FFB347",
  categories: ["Dhammapada", "Heart Sutra", "Lotus Sutra", "Diamond Sutra"],
  dailyGoal: 1,
  streakUnit: "passages",
  motivationalQuotes: [
    "Peace comes from within. Do not seek it without.",
    // ...
  ],
  icon: "dharma-wheel.png"
};
```

fitness-streak:

```javascript
const CONFIG = {
  appName: "Fitness Streak",
  primaryColor: "#DC143C",
  accentColor: "#FF4500",
  categories: ["Cardio", "Strength", "Flexibility", "Recovery"],
  dailyGoal: 1,
  streakUnit: "workouts",
  motivationalQuotes: [
    "The only bad workout is the one that didn't happen.",
    // ...
  ],
  icon: "dumbbell.png"
};
```

the template handles everything else: streak counting, calendar view, progress bars, localStorage persistence, PWA install prompt, offline mode.

## all 28 apps by category

### religious (6 core)
| App | Config focus | URL pattern |
|-----|-------------|-------------|
| scripture-streak | Bible reading | scripture-streak.surge.sh |
| quran-streak | Quran reading | quran-streak.surge.sh |
| torah-streak | Torah study | torah-streak.surge.sh |
| buddhist-streak | Buddhist texts | buddhist-streak.surge.sh |
| gita-streak | Bhagavad Gita | gita-streak.surge.sh |
| sikh-streak | Guru Granth Sahib | sikh-streak.surge.sh |

### non-religious (7)
| App | Config focus | URL pattern |
|-----|-------------|-------------|
| fitness-streak | workout tracking | fitness-streak.surge.sh |
| meditation-streak | meditation sessions | meditation-streak.surge.sh |
| reading-streak | book reading | reading-streak.surge.sh |
| coding-streak | daily coding | coding-streak.surge.sh |
| journal-streak | journaling | journal-streak.surge.sh |
| art-streak | creative practice | art-streak.surge.sh |
| language-streak | language learning | language-streak.surge.sh |

### denominational variants (14 extended)
| App | Config focus | URL pattern |
|-----|-------------|-------------|
| catholic-streak | Catholic devotionals | catholic-streak.surge.sh |
| orthodox-streak | Orthodox readings | orthodox-streak.surge.sh |
| protestant-streak | Protestant scripture | protestant-streak.surge.sh |
| baptist-streak | Baptist daily reading | baptist-streak.surge.sh |
| methodist-streak | Methodist devotions | methodist-streak.surge.sh |
| lutheran-streak | Lutheran readings | lutheran-streak.surge.sh |
| episcopal-streak | Episcopal lectionary | episcopal-streak.surge.sh |
| pentecostal-streak | Pentecostal devotions | pentecostal-streak.surge.sh |
| evangelical-streak | Evangelical reading | evangelical-streak.surge.sh |
| presbyterian-streak | Presbyterian readings | presbyterian-streak.surge.sh |
| anglican-streak | Anglican daily office | anglican-streak.surge.sh |
| mormon-streak | LDS scripture study | mormon-streak.surge.sh |
| sunni-streak | Sunni hadith study | sunni-streak.surge.sh |
| shia-streak | Shia text study | shia-streak.surge.sh |

plus sufi-streak in development.

## the deploy pipeline

generating a new variant takes about 8 minutes:

1. copy the template folder: `cp -r scripture-streak new-app-streak`
2. edit the CONFIG object in app.js (2 minutes)
3. update manifest.json with new name and colors (1 minute)
4. swap the icon (1 minute)
5. add denomination-specific quotes and categories (3 minutes)
6. deploy: `cd new-app-streak && surge .` (30 seconds)

done.

for the 14 denominational variants, i wrote a shell script that reads from a CSV of denomination configs and generates folders automatically:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
while IFS=, read -r name color accent categories icon; do
  cp -r base-template/ "${name}-streak/"
  sed -i '' "s/Scripture Streak/${name} Streak/g" "${name}-streak/app.js"
  sed -i '' "s/#2D5016/${color}/g" "${name}-streak/style.css"
  sed -i '' "s/#4A7C23/${accent}/g" "${name}-streak/style.css"
  cd "${name}-streak" && surge . "${name}-streak.surge.sh" && cd ..
done < denominations.csv
```

14 apps deployed in one terminal session. about 10 minutes total including the CSV setup.

## why templates beat custom builds

**speed.** 28 apps in 4 hours vs 28 apps in 28 days.

**consistency.** bug fix in the streak logic? fix it once in the template, regenerate. every app gets the fix.

**coverage.** the fitness-streak user and the quran-streak user want the exact same thing: a clean habit tracker. they just want different colors and categories. building 28 custom apps for that is insane.

**ASO/discoverability.** "catholic daily reading tracker" and "buddhist meditation streak" hit different search terms. same product, different keywords, different audiences.

## the tradeoffs

- if one app needs a unique feature (like prayer time integration for the Islamic apps), the template pattern breaks. you fork the template and maintain two versions.
- all 28 apps look similar. users who find multiple variants might wonder why they're separate.
- updating 28 deployed apps means running surge 28 times. i should probably automate that. (i haven't yet.)

the factory approach isn't for everything. it's for when the core logic is identical and only the content layer changes. habit tracking qualifies. a CRM does not.

if you have a working product and want to reach 10x more niches, consider what's actually different between variants. if it's just config, you don't need to rebuild. you need a better config file.

# DEV.TO ARTICLE 3

**PLATFORM:** dev.to / Hashnode / Medium
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Title:** comparison pages as an SEO strategy: build, deploy, rank
**Tags:** `seo` `webdev` `marketing` `html`
**Canonical URL:** (set on publish)
**Cover image alt:** search results page showing "X vs Y" comparison queries
**Target length:** 600-1000 words

---

"prayerlock vs hallow" gets searched. "focuslock vs opal" gets searched. "convertkit vs beehiiv" gets searched.

these are high-intent keywords with thin competition. someone searching "X vs Y" is already in buying mode. they've narrowed down to two options and want help deciding.

i built comparison pages targeting these queries. here's the template and why they rank.

## 3 live examples

**PrayerLock vs Hallow:** https://prayerlock-vs-hallow.surge.sh
targets the faith app comparison niche. compares features, pricing, offline support, and denomination-specific content.

**FocusLock vs Opal:** https://focuslock-vs-opal.surge.sh
targets the screen time/focus app comparison. compares blocking methods, scheduling, reporting, and pricing tiers.

**ConvertKit vs Beehiiv:** https://convertkit-vs-beehiiv.surge.sh
targets email platform comparison. compares pricing at different subscriber counts, automation features, deliverability, and monetization tools.

## why comparison pages rank

**high-intent keyword.** someone searching "convertkit vs beehiiv" has already done their initial research. they know both products. they want the tiebreaker. this is bottom-of-funnel traffic.

**thin competition.** big SaaS companies rarely build comparison pages against their competitors (legal concerns, brand risk). that leaves the field open for independent review sites and solo builders.

**natural link structure.** comparison content gets linked from forums, Reddit threads, and "best X" roundup posts. people share comparison pages because they answer a specific question.

**long-tail volume.** each comparison targets a specific 2-3 word phrase. individually these get 100-2,000 searches/month. but you can build 50 comparison pages covering 50 different matchups. the aggregate traffic adds up.

## the template structure

every comparison page follows this layout:

```html
<article itemscope itemtype="https://schema.org/Article">
  <h1>ProductA vs ProductB: honest comparison (2026)</h1>

  <section id="tldr">
    <h2>quick verdict</h2>
    <p>one-paragraph summary of who should pick which.</p>
  </section>

  <section id="comparison-table">
    <h2>feature comparison</h2>
    <table>
      <thead>
        <tr><th>Feature</th><th>ProductA</th><th>ProductB</th></tr>
      </thead>
      <tbody>
        <tr><td>Pricing</td><td>$X/mo</td><td>$Y/mo</td></tr>
        <tr><td>Free tier</td><td>Yes/No</td><td>Yes/No</td></tr>
        <!-- 8-12 comparison rows -->
      </tbody>
    </table>
  </section>

  <section id="deep-dive">
    <h2>detailed breakdown</h2>
    <!-- 3-4 subsections comparing specific categories -->
  </section>

  <section id="verdict">
    <h2>which one should you pick</h2>
    <p>scenario-based recommendation.</p>
  </section>
</article>
```

## Schema.org markup

adding structured data to comparison pages helps search engines understand the content type:

```html
<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "ProductA vs ProductB: honest comparison",
  "author": {
    "@type": "Person",
    "name": "Your Name"
  },
  "datePublished": "2026-03-01",
  "dateModified": "2026-03-07",
  "description": "Side-by-side comparison of ProductA and ProductB covering pricing, features, and use cases."
}
```

you can also use the `Review` and `Product` schema types to get rich snippets in search results. this makes your listing stand out with star ratings and pricing info directly in the SERP.

## the build process

1. pick a comparison that people actually search for. use Google autocomplete: type "productname vs" and see what comes up.
2. research both products. features, pricing, limitations. be honest about both.
3. write the quick verdict first. this is what people want immediately.
4. build the comparison table. 8-12 rows covering the things people actually care about.
5. write 3-4 detailed sections going deeper on the most important differences.
6. add Schema.org markup.
7. deploy: `surge . productA-vs-productB.surge.sh`

time per page: about 45 minutes for research and writing. 5 minutes for markup and deploy.

## monetization angle

comparison pages are natural affiliate opportunities. if you're in an affiliate program for either product, your recommendation carries real weight because the reader is already sold on one of two options. you're just nudging them toward the one with your affiliate link.

without affiliate programs, comparison pages still drive traffic to your other content. add an email capture ("get my full comparison spreadsheet of 20 email tools") and build your list from high-intent visitors.

the strategy: pick a niche with 20+ competing products. build comparison pages for the top 50 matchups. each page targets a unique long-tail keyword. compound the traffic over 3-6 months.

# LINKEDIN POST 1

**PLATFORM:** LinkedIn
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Target length:** 150-300 words
**Angle:** honest reflection on overbuilding

---

170 websites. 33 days. $0 revenue. Here's what I learned.

I spent a month building. Every day, something new shipped. PWAs, landing pages, SaaS tools, comparison sites, content calendars. The output was real. The numbers were real. The revenue was not.

Three things became clear:

1. Building is the comfort zone. Writing the cold email, listing the product, posting on LinkedIn, doing the outreach. That's where the friction lives. Most builders (myself included) avoid friction by building more.

2. Distribution has no template. I can generate 28 apps from one template in 4 hours. I cannot generate 28 distribution channels that fast. Distribution is manual, messy, and slow. There's no `surge .` for getting attention.

3. The ratio is wrong. I had 170 deployed sites and 0 distribution systems. The correct ratio is probably 1:3. For every thing you build, spend 3x the time getting it in front of people.

The bottleneck was never building. The bottleneck was listing, emailing, posting, and following up.

Now I'm working backwards. Going through 170 sites and asking: which 10 of these could generate revenue this week if I just listed them properly?

That's the real question. Not "what should I build next" but "what's already built that nobody knows about."

What would you prioritize: building the next thing or distributing what already exists?

---

---

---

the $0 SaaS stack: 10 free tools I built and deployed with zero backend infrastructure.

No servers. No databases. No monthly bills. Every tool runs entirely in the browser.

Here's the full list:

- SiteScore (sitescore-free.surge.sh) - website auditor
- PageScorer (pagescorer-app.surge.sh) - page speed scorer
- ColdMaxx (coldmaxx-app.surge.sh) - cold email toolkit
- InvoiceForge (invoiceforge-app.surge.sh) - invoice generator
- ContentCalendar (contentcalendar.surge.sh) - content planner
- Social Dashboard (social-dashboard-pm.surge.sh) - social metrics
- ROI Calculator (roicalc-app.surge.sh) - marketing ROI math
- ProspectMaxx (prospectmaxx-app.surge.sh) - prospect list builder
- StackMaxx (stackmaxx-app.surge.sh) - tech stack planner
- PitchDeck Builder (pitchdeck-app.surge.sh) - pitch deck creator

The stack: vanilla HTML, CSS, JavaScript. No React. No Next.js. No npm install.

Data lives in localStorage. PWA manifest makes each tool installable. Service workers handle offline mode.

The insight that changed how I build: most single-user tools don't need a backend. A calculator doesn't need a database. A template generator doesn't need auth. An invoice builder doesn't need a server.

The cheapest infrastructure is no infrastructure.

Total hosting cost across all 10 tools: $0/mo.
Total build time: about 3 days.
Total server maintenance: zero, forever.

Try any of them. The URLs are all live.

# LINKEDIN POST 3

**PLATFORM:** LinkedIn
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Target length:** 150-300 words
**Angle:** demo-first local biz selling

---

I built websites for 8 local businesses before talking to any of them.

Dental office. Barber shop. Auto repair. Pizza place. Plumber. Landscaper. Salon. Dog groomer.

Each one is a fully working site: services, pricing, contact form, Google Maps embed, mobile responsive. Deployed to surge.sh, ready to show.

The pitch is simple:

"Hey [business name], I noticed your current site is [slow/outdated/doesn't exist]. I already built you a new one. Here's the link: [dental-demo.surge.sh]. If you want it on your real domain, it's $X."

The conversion math makes sense. The business owner doesn't have to imagine what they'd get. They can see it. They can tap through it on their phone right now.

Traditional web dev pitch: "I can build you a website. Here's my portfolio of other people's sites. It'll take 2-3 weeks."

Demo-first pitch: "I already built your website. Look at it. Want it live on your domain by Friday?"

The time investment per demo site is about 90 minutes. Template layout with the business info swapped in. Not custom design. Functional and clean.

If 1 out of 8 says yes at $500, that's $500 for 12 hours of total work. About $42/hr. Not bad for cold outreach with a tangible deliverable instead of a pitch deck.

Has anyone tried this approach? I'm curious about close rates on demo-first vs portfolio-first pitches.

---

---

# LINKEDIN POST 4

**PLATFORM:** LinkedIn
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Target length:** 150-300 words
**Angle:** factory vs artisan philosophy

---

Factory vs artisan approach to software.

I built 28 habit-tracking apps from a single template in one afternoon. Same streak logic, same localStorage persistence, same PWA setup. Different colors, different categories, different names.

scripture-streak for Bible readers. quran-streak for Quran study. fitness-streak for gym habits. coding-streak for daily programming practice. 14 denominational variants from Catholic to Sunni.

28 deployed apps from 1 template. About 8 minutes per variant.

The artisan approach would be: research each niche for a week. Design custom UX for each audience. Build 28 separate codebases. Ship one every 2 weeks. Take 14 months.

The factory approach: build the template right once. Swap the config. Deploy. Repeat.

Factory wins when the core logic is identical. Streak tracking is streak tracking. The Catholic user and the coding user both want the same thing: a counter that goes up when they show up. The wrapper changes. The engine doesn't.

Factory loses when each product genuinely needs different logic. A CRM for dentists and a CRM for law firms need different workflows, not different colors.

The question to ask: "What's actually different between these variants?"

If the answer is just content, colors, and names, use the factory. If the answer involves different data models, different user flows, or different business rules, go artisan.

Most people default to artisan when factory would work. That's why they ship 1 app in 3 months instead of 28 in one day.

---

---

# LINKEDIN POST 5

**PLATFORM:** LinkedIn
**STATUS:** PENDING_REVIEW
**Target length:** 150-300 words
**Angle:** comparison page SEO playbook

---

The comparison page playbook (free).

Someone searches "convertkit vs beehiiv." They're not browsing. They've already narrowed to two options. They want help picking one.

That's bottom-of-funnel traffic. comparison queries convert at 3-5x the rate of informational keywords.

Here's why comparison pages work and how to build them:

1. Pick matchups people actually search. Type "productname vs" into Google and read the autocomplete suggestions. Those are real search queries with real volume.

2. Build an honest comparison. Feature table with 8-12 rows. Quick verdict at the top. Detailed breakdown in the middle. "Pick this if..." recommendations at the bottom.

3. Add Schema.org markup. Article schema + Product schema gets your listing rich snippets in search results. Stars, pricing, review counts directly in the SERP.

4. Deploy. I use surge.sh. One command, live in 30 seconds. prayerlock-vs-hallow.surge.sh is a real example.

5. Build 20-50 pages across your niche. Each page targets a unique long-tail keyword. Individually they might get 200 searches/month. Collectively across 50 pages, that's 10,000 targeted visits.

Why this works for solo builders: big SaaS companies almost never build comparison pages against their competitors. Legal teams get nervous. Brand teams say no. That leaves the SERP wide open for independent reviewers.

Three examples I deployed:
- prayerlock-vs-hallow.surge.sh (faith apps)
- focuslock-vs-opal.surge.sh (focus/screen time)
- convertkit-vs-beehiiv.surge.sh (email platforms)

Time per page: about 45 minutes.

DM me if you want the HTML template. It's free.

# DISTRIBUTION NOTES

## dev.to posting order
1. Article 1 (10 SaaS tools) - strongest standalone hook
2. Article 2 (streak factory) - best technical story
3. Article 3 (comparison SEO) - most actionable for dev.to audience

## cross-posting
- Hashnode: cross-post all 3, set canonical to dev.to
- Medium: cross-post all 3, set canonical to dev.to
- Space 1-2 days between each to avoid flooding

## LinkedIn posting schedule

- Post 2 (Wednesday) - tool list with URLs for clicks
- Post 3 (Thursday) - local biz angle for small biz LinkedIn crowd
- Post 4 (Friday) - philosophical/strategy angle
